cards.title: Cold Friction Bath (Abhyanga + Cold Affusion)
cards._legacyHtml:
Indication: Fever reduction, skin toning, nervous system stimulation, sluggish circulation.
Temperature: 15–20°C (cold)
Duration: 5–10 minutes
Method: Wet rough towel with cold water. Vigorously rub each body segment — upper limbs → lower limbs → trunk → back. Re-wet as needed. Patient stands or sits on stool. Follow with dry towelling.
Physiological effect: Initial vasoconstriction → rebound vasodilation → ↑ circulation, ↑ BMR, sympathetic stimulation.
Contraindications: Arteriosclerosis, Raynaud's, severe HTN, cardiac disease, elderly frail patients.

cards.title: Hot Foot Bath
cards._legacyHtml:
Indication: Common cold, headache, insomnia, pelvic congestion, menstrual cramps, early fever (derivative effect).
Temperature: 40–45°C
Duration: 15–20 minutes
Method: Patient seated. Both feet immersed to ankle in hot water tub. Cover with blanket. Apply cold compress to forehead. Add mustard powder (1 tsp) for extra stimulation. End with cold splash or cold friction.
Physiological effect: Derivative hyperaemia — draws blood from congested areas (head, pelvis) to periphery.
Contraindications: Peripheral neuropathy, T1DM with vascular complications, varicose veins (severe), loss of sensation in feet.

cards.title: Spinal Bath
cards._legacyHtml:
Indication: Hypertension, functional nervous disorders, fatigue, backache, insomnia, psychosomatic disorders.
Temperature: 26–32°C (neutral-cold)
Duration: 20–30 minutes
Method: Patient lies supine in shallow trough filled with water covering only the spine (T1–sacrum). Head and limbs out of water. No movement. Quiet rest. Temperature maintained with top-ups.
Physiological effect: Tonic effect on sympathetic ganglia chain. ↓ SNS activity → ↓ BP, ↓ cortisol, muscle relaxation.
Contraindications: Acute infection, open wounds, severe cardiac disease, post-surgery.

cards.title: Immersion Bath (Full Bath)
cards._legacyHtml:
Indication: Fever, skin diseases, muscle spasm, arthritis, obesity, metabolic disorders.
Temperatures: Cold: 10–18°C | Cool: 18–26°C | Neutral: 33–36°C | Hot: 37–40°C | Very Hot: 40–45°C
Duration: 3–20 min depending on temperature
Method: Patient fully immersed to neck. Time precisely by temperature (cold: 3–5 min). Exit and dry promptly. Never leave patient unattended in hot or cold bath.
Neutral bath (33–36°C): Best for anxiety, psychiatric disorders, pain — profoundly sedating.
Contraindications: Epilepsy (unsupervised), severe cardiac, post-MI, malignancy (hot baths), menstruation (hot bath).

cards.title: Sitz Bath (Hip Bath)
cards._legacyHtml:
Indication:
• Cold sitz: Pelvic congestion, constipation, haemorrhoids, menstrual irregularity, prostate disorders, urinary incontinence
• Hot sitz: Dysmenorrhoea, prostatitis, anal fissure (muscle relaxation)
• Alternate sitz: Pelvic inflammatory disease, chronic constipation
Temperature: Cold: 10–20°C | Hot: 40–45°C
Duration: Cold: 3–10 min | Hot: 10–20 min
Method: Special sitz tub (or two tubs for alternate). Water covers hips/perineum only. Feet in footbath of opposite temperature for derivative effect.
Contraindications: Active bleeding per rectum, pregnancy (hot), acute UTI (hot), vascular disease.

cards.title: Steam Bath (Cabinet Steam)
cards._legacyHtml:
Indication: Obesity, skin diseases, arthritis, common cold, detoxification, poor circulation, respiratory congestion.
Temperature: 43–48°C steam
Duration: 10–20 minutes
Method: Patient seated in steam cabinet, head outside. Apply cold compress to head throughout. Monitor pulse. After steam: cool shower or cold friction. Never steam >20 min or in patients with uncontrolled HTN.
Physiological effects: ↑ core temperature → profuse sweating → ↑ skin circulation → toxin elimination via skin → ↑ BMR 20–30%
Contraindications: Uncontrolled HTN, cardiac disease, epilepsy, pregnancy, anaemia, hypoglycaemia, T2DM poorly controlled, active TB.

cards.title: Wet Sheet Pack (Full Pack)
cards._legacyHtml:
Indication: High fever, acute infections, detoxification, obesity, psychosomatic disorders, insomnia.
Temperature stages:
1. Cold/Tonic (5–15 min): Stimulating, ↑ BMR — fever reduction
2. Neutral (15–30 min): Sedating, sweating — insomnia, anxiety
3. Hot (30–45 min): Eliminative, profuse sweating — detox, obesity
Method: Sheet wrung from cold water. Patient wrapped head-to-toe tightly. Outer dry blanket. Monitor temperature and pulse every 5 min. Cold compress to head in hot stage.
Contraindications: Extreme debility, claustrophobia, cardiac failure.

cards.title: Liver Pack (Hepatic Compress)
cards._legacyHtml:
Indication: NAFLD, hepatitis (subacute), liver congestion, dyspepsia, biliary dyskinesia.
Temperature: Hot (40–44°C)
Duration: 20–30 minutes followed by 5-min cold compress
Method: Hot wet towel folded to cover right upper quadrant (T6–L1 dermatome). Cover with dry towel. Replace every 5 min as it cools. End with cold compress for 3 min.
Physiological effect: ↑ hepatic blood flow, ↓ hepatic capsular tension, smooth muscle relaxation of bile ducts, ↑ bile production.
Clinical note: Avoid in acute hepatitis with fever. Always combine with appropriate diet modification.

cards.title: Abdominal Mud Pack
cards._legacyHtml:
Indication: Constipation, IBS, indigestion, hyperacidity, peptic ulcer (subacute), abdominal obesity, hepatomegaly.
Type of mud: Black cotton soil or multani mitti (fullers earth) — cool application
Temperature: Room temperature or refrigerator-cooled (not freezing)
Application area: Navel to pubic symphysis (or umbilicus to lower ribs for liver involvement)
Duration: 20–30 minutes
Timing: Morning before breakfast or minimum 3 hours after meals
Physiological effects: Absorbs heat and inflammatory mediators from intestinal wall → ↓ bowel inflammation → ↑ peristalsis → ↓ sympathetic tone in mesenteric plexus
Contraindications: Acute appendicitis, active GI bleeding, strangulated hernia.

cards.title: Mud Eye Pack
cards._legacyHtml:
Indication: Eye strain, conjunctivitis (non-infectious), digital eye fatigue, early glaucoma (adjunct), headache from eye strain, dark circles.
Mud preparation: Fine-textured clean mud (fullers earth preferred) mixed to thick paste with cold water
Temperature: Cool — refrigerated for 10 min before application
Application: Applied over closed eyelids in oval pads, 0.5 cm thick. No pressure on eyeballs.
Duration: 20–30 minutes. Patient lies supine.
Physiological effects: Cooling reduces intraocular pressure and pericorneal vascular congestion. Drawing action of mud reduces periorbital oedema.
Contraindications: Active eye infection, open wounds around eyes, recent eye surgery.

cards.title: Mud Bath (Full Body)
cards._legacyHtml:
Indication: Psoriasis, eczema, generalised arthritis, metabolic syndrome, obesity, fibromyalgia.
Mud used: Black cotton soil or marine mud — rich in trace minerals (Fe, Mg, Zn, Si)
Temperature: Warm (33–38°C) for musculoskeletal; cool (25–30°C) for skin diseases
Duration: 20–45 minutes
Method: Patient lies or sits in mud tub or specially prepared mud pit. Mud applied 2–3 cm thick. Head and face clear. After: warm shower, rest 30 min.
Physiological effects: Mineral absorption through skin, anti-inflammatory peloid effect, mechanical pressure effect similar to hydrotherapy, ↓ cortisol, ↑ skin barrier repair.
Contraindications: Infected skin lesions, renal failure (mineral overload risk), hyperthyroidism, malignancy.

cards.title: Mud Spinal Pack
cards._legacyHtml:
Indication: Hypertension, spondylitis, chronic backache, stress, sympathetic nervous system hyperactivity.
Application area: Full length of spine (C2 to sacrum), 4 cm wide strip
Temperature: Cool (RT or slightly chilled)
Duration: 30–45 minutes
Method: Patient lies prone. Mud strip applied directly on spine, covered with wet cloth then dry towel. After: gentle wipe and rest.
Physiological effects: Cool mud over sympathetic ganglia chain → ↓ SNS tone → ↓ BP, ↓ muscle tension, ↓ cortisol. Same derivation as spinal bath but sustained contact.
Contraindications: Scoliosis with skin breakdown, acute disc herniation with radiculopathy, open wounds.

cards.title: Effleurage (Stroking Massage)
cards._legacyHtml:
Technique: Long, rhythmic, gliding strokes with full palm and fingers. Direction always centripetal (towards heart).
Pressure: Light (superficial) → moderate (general) → firm (deep lymphatic)
Duration: 5–10 min per region
Oil: Sesame (Vata), Coconut (Pitta, summer), Mustard (Kapha, winter), Neem (skin infections)
Physiological effects: ↑ venous/lymphatic return, ↓ muscle tension, activates parasympathetic, ↓ substance P (pain mediator), ↑ serotonin
Indications: Opening and closing technique for all massage, lymphoedema, anxiety, fatigue, poor circulation
Contraindications: Acute inflammation, DVT, skin infections, malignancy over area.

cards.title: Petrissage (Kneading)
cards._legacyHtml:
Technique: Rhythmic compression, lifting, and rolling of muscle bellies between fingers and thumb or both hands. Includes kneading, wringing, picking-up movements.
Pressure: Moderate to firm
Speed: 1–2 cycles per second
Physiological effects: ↑ muscle blood flow 3–4x, ↑ lymphatic drainage, breakdown of fibrous adhesions, ↑ removal of metabolic waste (lactic acid), trigger point release
Indications: Muscle stiffness, DOMS, chronic myalgia, fibromyalgia, constipation (abdominal kneading), obesity
Contraindications: Acute muscle tears, bruising, phlebitis, osteoporosis (vigorous petrissage).

cards.title: Vibration & Tapotement
cards._legacyHtml:
Vibration: Fine trembling applied through fingertips or palm. Manually or with vibrator.
• Effect: ↓ nerve irritability, stimulate peristalsis, loosen secretions (chest vibration for COPD/asthma)
Tapotement (Percussion): Rhythmic beating — includes hacking (ulnar edge), cupping (cupped hand), clapping, tapping (fingertips)
• Effect: ↑ muscle tone, reflex stimulation, chest physiotherapy (loosens mucus plugs in bronchiectasis, COPD)
Duration: 2–3 min per technique
Indications: Respiratory disease (chest), muscle atony, nerve paralysis, scar tissue breaking
Contraindications: Osteoporosis, thin skin, acute inflammation, directly over kidneys.

cards.title: Abdominal Massage Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Indication: Constipation, IBS, bloating, NAFLD (adjunct), sluggish bowel, colonic dysmotility
Position: Supine, knees slightly bent. Warm oil applied.
Sequence:
1. Effleurage (5 min clockwise): Wide circular strokes following colon direction
2. Petrissage sigmoid area (left iliac fossa): Kneading and picking up
3. Petrissage transverse colon (umbilical region): Wringing
4. Vibration along colon path: stimulate peristalsis
5. Tapotement (light): ascending → transverse → descending colon
6. Closing effleurage (2 min clockwise)
Duration: 20–25 min total
Timing: Before breakfast or 3h post-meal
Contraindications: Acute appendicitis, active GI bleeding, pregnancy, hernia, recent abdominal surgery.

cards.title: Therapeutic Fasting — Types & Selection
cards._legacyHtml:
1. Total Fast (Water Fast): Water only. 24–72 hrs supervised. Indications: acute conditions, severe toxaemia, obesity with full monitoring.
2. Partial Fast (Fruit / Juice Fast): Fruit juices 200–300 mL every 2 hrs. 3–21 days. Most common BNYS prescription. Indications: metabolic syndrome, NCD reversal, skin diseases.
3. Mono Diet Fast: Single food (coconut water, buttermilk, grapes). 5–14 days. More easily tolerated.
4. Intermittent Fasting (IF):
• 16:8 TRE: Evidence A for T2DM, metabolic syndrome, PCOS
• 5:2 protocol: Weight management
• Alternate day: Obesity, insulin resistance
Healing crisis (expected days 1–2): Headache, fatigue, coated tongue, bad breath — signs of active detox, NOT side effects.
Absolute contraindications: T1DM, active TB, Hb <8 g/dL, anorexia nervosa, pregnancy, active cardiac disease, age <15 or >75 (unsupervised).

cards.title: Fasting Monitoring Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Daily monitoring parameters during supervised fast:
• Vitals (BP, HR, temperature) morning and evening
• Blood glucose (especially first 3 days and if symptomatic)
• Urine output and ketone level (expected by day 2–3)
• Tongue coat assessment (healing crisis indicator)
• Bowel movement (enema may be required on day 2 and 4)
• Mood and energy level (patient diary)

Break fast properly:
Day 1: Diluted fresh fruit juice (100 mL q2h)
Day 2: Fresh fruit (banana, papaya, apple) — small amounts
Day 3: Cooked vegetables, soft khichdi
Day 4: Normal light sattvic diet
Never break a fast with heavy food — risk of refeeding syndrome.

cards.title: Sattvic Diet Principles (BNYS Therapeutic Diet)
cards._legacyHtml:
Core principles:
• Plant-predominant, minimally processed, seasonal, locally sourced
• Alkaline-forming: All fruits (even citrus — alkaline ash), all vegetables, sprouts, millet, seeds
• Acid-forming (limit): Refined carbs, sugar, alcohol, excess dairy, meat

Therapeutic ratios:
• 70% alkaline-forming foods : 30% acid-forming
• Raw:Cooked = 50:50 ideally (adjust for digestive capacity)

Timing principle:
• Breakfast: 7–9 AM (Kapha time — lighter)
• Lunch: 12–2 PM (Pitta time — main meal)
• Dinner: 6–7 PM (light, warm, cooked)
• Nothing after 8 PM

Key therapeutic foods: Sprouts (enzymatic), coconut water (electrolytes), bitter gourd (glucose regulation), amla (vit C, antioxidant), turmeric (anti-inflammatory), flaxseed (omega-3, hormonal balance).

cards.title: Chromotherapy (Colour Therapy) — Clinical Guide
cards._legacyHtml:
Principle: Each colour has a specific wavelength and photobiological effect. Applied via coloured glass, cellophane, LED lamps, or solarised water.

Red (620–750 nm): Stimulant, ↑ circulation, ↑ haemoglobin. Indications: anaemia, paralysis, hypothyroidism, fatigue. Contraindicated: HTN, fever, inflammation.
Orange (590–620 nm): ↑ appetite, ↑ lymphatic, reproductive tonic. Indications: menstrual disorders, PCOS, digestive weakness, depression.
Yellow (570–590 nm): Nerve stimulant, ↑ digestive enzymes, anti-diabetic effect. Indications: constipation, skin diseases, DM, nerve weakness.
Green (495–570 nm): Balancing, antiseptic, anti-inflammatory. Indications: HTN, cardiac, ulcers, headache. Safest for prolonged use.
Blue (450–495 nm): Sedative, antipyretic, anti-inflammatory. Indications: fever, insomnia, hyperthyroidism, anxiety, skin infections.
Violet/Indigo (380–450 nm): Antiseptic, pain-relieving, haemostatic. Indications: eye diseases, migraine, spiritual calm.

Solarised water: Water in coloured glass bottle placed in sunlight for 4–6 hours. Dose: 30–60 mL TID.

cards.title: Magnet Therapy — Clinical Application
cards._legacyHtml:
Types:
• Static magnets: Permanent neodymium or ceramic magnets
• PEMF (Pulsed Electromagnetic Field): 0.5–100 Hz pulsed devices

Pole applications:
• North pole (negative): Sedating, anti-inflammatory, pain-reducing, anti-infective. Used for acute conditions, infections, pain, cancer adjunct.
• South pole (positive): Stimulating, warming, circulation-promoting. Used for chronic degenerative, paralysis, atrophy.
• Bipolar (both poles): Used for fracture healing, wound healing, general tonic.

Magnetic water: Water magnetised by placing on north pole for 4+ hours. Dose: 60–120 mL BD. Used for kidney stones, UTI, arthritis.

Treatment duration: 20–30 min per session, once or twice daily. Course: 2–4 weeks.
Contraindications: Pacemaker, implanted electronic devices, pregnancy, during menstruation (lower abdomen), malignancy (direct stimulating pole).

cards.title: Acupressure — Key Clinical Points
cards._legacyHtml:
Pressure technique: Thumb or index finger. Firm perpendicular pressure until Deqi sensation (ache/heaviness/tingling). Hold 30–60 seconds per point. 2–3 times per session.

Master points:
• LI4 (Hegu): Dorsum of hand, 1st–2nd metacarpal junction. For headache, face, pain, immunity, labour induction (contraindicated in pregnancy)
• ST36 (Zusanli): 3 cun below patella, lateral to tibial crest. Master digestive point — IBS, immunity, energy, nausea
• PC6 (Neiguan): 2 cun proximal to wrist crease, between tendons. For nausea, anxiety, cardiac palpitations, motion sickness
• SP6 (Sanyinjiao): 3 cun above medial malleolus. For menstrual disorders, PCOS, insomnia, urinary disorders. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
• KD1 (Yongquan): Centre of sole, proximal third. For HTN, insomnia, anxiety, reviving consciousness
• GB21 (Jianjing): Midpoint of trapezius. For neck/shoulder tension, headache. Contraindicated in pregnancy.
• GV20 (Baihui): Crown of head. For mental clarity, concentration, HTN.
• BL32 (Ciliao): 2nd sacral foramen. For lower backache, sciatica, dysmenorrhoea.

cards.title: Vamana (Therapeutic Emesis)
cards._legacyHtml:
Dosha: Kapha
Purvakarma: Snehana (internal oleation with ghee 3–7 days) + Svedana (sudation). Night before: Kapha-aggravating diet (curd, heavy foods).
Procedure: Morning on empty stomach. Administer 1–1.5L Yashtimadhu kashaya + honey. Emesis expected 4–8 times (Pravara=8, Madhyama=6, Heena=4).
Indications: Chronic asthma, bronchitis, eczema, psoriasis, T2DM (early), obesity, chronic rhinitis, epilepsy.
Paschatkarma: Samsarjana karma (graded diet) — peya (thin rice gruel) Day 1 → vilepi (thick gruel) Day 2 → akrita yusha Day 3 → krita yusha Day 4 → solid food Day 5–7.
Contraindications: Age <7 or >70, pregnancy, emaciation, cardiac disease, hiatal hernia, active upper GI bleed.

cards.title: Virechana (Therapeutic Purgation)
cards._legacyHtml:
Dosha: Pitta
Purvakarma: 3–7 days internal oleation (Ghrita) + Svedana. Deepana-pachana herbs first to kindle Agni.
Procedure: Trivrit churna 10–30 g or Haritaki in warm water morning on empty stomach. Complete purgation expected: 10–30 times (Pravara), 6–10 times (Madhyama), 2–5 times (Heena).
Indications: Liver disorders, jaundice, skin diseases (psoriasis, urticaria), PCOS, gout, IBD, hyperacidity, chronic constipation.
Paschatkarma: Same Samsarjana karma as Vamana. Avoid hot, spicy, sour foods for 7 days.
Contraindications: Dehydration, active rectal bleeding, rectal prolapse, post-surgery (recent), severe diarrhoea, extreme debility.

cards.title: Basti (Therapeutic Enema)
cards._legacyHtml:
Dosha: Vata — 'King of all Panchakarma'
Types:
• Anuvasana Basti: Oil enema — 60–120 mL warm sesame or Dashamoola oil. Retained indefinitely. For pure Vata conditions.
• Kashaya (Niruha) Basti: Decoction enema — 480 mL. Components: honey (60 mL) + rock salt (5 g) + sesame oil (60 mL) + herbal paste + decoction. Retained 30–48 min.

Indications: Vata disorders — paralysis, constipation, arthritis, sciatica, infertility, IBS, neuromuscular diseases.
Administration: Left lateral position. Lukewarm. Slow administration. Supine for 30 min post-basti. Light warm diet after.
Contraindications: Active haemorrhoids (bleeding), anal fissure, diarrhoea, ascites, Prameha (DM) without supervision, pregnancy.

cards.title: Nasya (Nasal Administration)
cards._legacyHtml:
Dosha: Vata and Kapha of head region
Purvakarma: Local facial Abhyanga + facial steam for 3 days.
Procedure: Patient supine, neck hyperextended, eyes closed. 6–8 drops of medicated oil per nostril (Anu taila for Vata, Shadbindu taila for Kapha). Inhale deeply. Spit out oropharyngeal secretions. Repeat for 7 days.
Oils: Anu taila (general), Shadbindu taila (sinusitis), Brahmi ghrita (mental clarity), plain sesame oil (maintenance).
Indications: Chronic sinusitis, migraine, hair loss, eye and ear disorders, cervical spondylosis, facial palsy, nasal polyps, premature greying, memory improvement.
Paschatkarma: Avoid cold air, cold water, dust for 3 hrs. No heavy meal immediately after.
Contraindications: After meals, pregnancy (1st trimester), active fever, epistaxis.
